Title: Gene Expression and Silencing Studies in Phytophthora infestans Reveal Infection-Specific Nutrient Transporters and a Role for the Nitrate Reductase Pathway in Plant Pathogenesis

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Major Facilitator Superfamily; high-affinity NRT2 nitrate transporter
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Strongly induced during leaf infection and functionally associated with nitrate assimilation/virulence; cluster silencing impairs leaf infection and increases nitrate toxicity.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Average 70-fold induced in leaves versus artificial media in Experiment Two and 200-fold in Experiment One; low in tubers, germinated cysts, and artificial media; peaks around 3–4 dpi in leaves.
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ham34 promoter indirectly used via sense-NR silencing construct that co-silenced the cluster
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane (inferred for NRT2 family transporter)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports nitrogen acquisition and may reduce nitrate toxicity during host colonization.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf versus tuber infection; nitrate and ammonium amendments; host organ differences
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Down-regulated by regional DNA-directed RNAi spreading from the targeted NR locus across the nitrate assimilation cluster.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of cluster expression associates with nitrate hypersensitivity and severe leaf pathogenicity defect.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; co-silencing in NR sense transformants; growth assays on nitrate medium; infection assays
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NRT is a high-affinity nitrate transporter in the nitrate assimilation gene cluster. 2. It is among the most strongly leaf-induced MFS genes during infection. 3. NRT is coexpressed with NR and NiR and is preferentially expressed in leaves rather than tubers. 4. Cluster silencing suppresses NRT expression and correlates with loss of leaf pathogenicity. 5. Residual nitrate uptake likely persists via other transporters, but NRT/cluster function contributes to coping with host nitrate.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential pathogen control target linked to nitrate-rich host environments and fertilizer-associated disease severity.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors discuss possible roles in nitrate uptake and/or nitrate efflux; exact transport direction in P. infestans was not directly tested. Another nitrate transporter, PITG_09342, showed opposite expression behavior and was not functionally analyzed here.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq (accession NA in provided text); metabolite profiling

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Co-induced with NRT and NR during leaf infection; co-silenced with NR and associated with loss of leaf pathogenicity and nitrate tolerance.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low in tubers and artificial media; sharply induced in infected leaves with peak around 3–4 dpi.
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ham34 promoter indirectly via NR sense construct causing cluster silencing
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides reduced nitrogen for metabolism and contributes to nitrate detoxification pathway.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf versus tuber infection; nitrate and ammonium amendments
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Down-regulated by spreading DNA-directed RNAi/heterochromatinization across the nitrate assimilation cluster.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Cluster silencing causes virulence defect on leaves and increased nitrate sensitivity.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; co-silencing analysis in NR-targeted transformants; infection assays; nitrate growth assays
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NiR is the third gene in the nitrate assimilation cluster with NRT and NR. 2. NiR is coordinately induced during leaf infection and weakly expressed in tubers/media. 3. NiR is strongly co-silenced when NR is targeted by DNA-directed RNAi. 4. The cluster is important for successful colonization of nitrate-rich leaf tissue. 5. Results support a role in assimilatory reduction/detoxification of nitrate during infection.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential anti-oomycete target as part of a compact virulence-associated nitrate assimilation module.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct catalytic activity was inferred from annotation and cluster context; individual NiR-specific knockdown was not performed in this paper.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq (accession NA in provided text); metabolite profiling

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Early infection-induced transporter likely involved in amino acid acquisition during biotrophic growth.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Very low in sporangia; peaks at 1.5–2.5 dpi in infected tomato leaves, then declines sharply.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nitrogen and carbon source acquisition for pathogen growth.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Early leaf infection compared with sporangia and later infection stages
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PITG_12808 is one of the AAAP transporters most strongly induced during early infection. 2. Expression peaks during early biotrophy and falls as necrotrophy marker Npp1 rises. 3. It represents infection-specific nutrient transporter deployment in P. infestans.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for disrupting early nutrient uptake during infection.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Early infection-induced transporter likely contributing to amino acid uptake during initial host colonization.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Very low in sporangia; peaks at 1.5–2.5 dpi in infected tomato leaves and then declines.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PITG_17803 is strongly induced in planta during early infection. 2. Expression coincides with or slightly precedes the biotrophic marker Avr3A. 3. Transcript abundance drops as infection progresses toward necrotrophy.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate marker/target for early infection-specific nutrient uptake.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper text also mentions PITG_17804 in Discussion as an ortholog comparison, but core experimental validation was for PITG_17803.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Early infection-induced amino acid transporter candidate associated with biotrophic nutrient uptake.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Very low in sporangia; peaks between 1.5 and 2.5 dpi in leaves, then declines precipitously.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PITG_20230 is one of three AAAP genes validated as highly induced in early infection. 2. Its expression is temporally associated with the biotrophic phase. 3. Transcript levels decrease as necrotrophy begins.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to impair early nutrient uptake during host colonization.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces nitrate to nitrite as part of assimilatory nitrogen metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supplies reduced nitrogen for amino acid biosynthesis and helps alleviate nitrate toxicity during host colonization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for full pathogenicity on leaves; silencing NR together with the linked nitrate assimilation cluster abolishes effective leaf colonization and increases nitrate sensitivity.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly induced during leaf infection, peaking around 3–4 dpi in tomato leaves and 2–3 dpi in potato leaves; low in tubers and artificial media.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ham34 promoter (constitutive; used to express sense NR for silencing)
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step supported by strong infection-specific induction and silencing phenotype, but not explicitly proven rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf versus tuber infection; nitrate and ammonium amendments; light/dark regime; humidity; sporulation-related culture age
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: DNA-directed RNAi silencing via expression of a sense NR transgene; prior work and this paper indicate heterochromatinization of the target locus, with spreading silencing across the adjacent NRT-NR-NiR cluster.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing causes severe loss of leaf pathogenicity and increased nitrate toxicity; pathway activity may depend on nutrient context.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; stable transgenic silencing; chlorate sensitivity assay; infection phenotyping on leaves and tubers; growth assay on nitrate-amended medium; qPCR biomass quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NR is part of a physically linked nitrate assimilation cluster with NRT and NiR. 2. NR is strongly induced during leaf infection but not tuber infection or most media conditions. 3. Sense-transgene-mediated silencing of NR down-regulates NR plus adjacent NRT and NiR. 4. Silenced strains are nearly nonpathogenic on leaves but only slightly impaired on tubers. 5. Silenced strains are hypersensitive to nitrate, supporting a role in nitrate detoxification/assimilation during pathogenesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suggests nitrate assimilation capacity is a virulence-enabling vulnerability in P. infestans; targeting NR/cluster function could help disease control, especially under high-nitrate fertilization conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Silencing spread beyond NR to the adjacent cluster genes but not to flanking genes PITG_13010 and PITG_13014; P. infestans cannot use nitrate as sole nitrogen source despite retaining this pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq (this study; accession NA in provided text); metabolite profiling of nitrate/ammonium/sugars/amino acids
